entries.title: Where LaTeX injection occurs
Reading a file with LaTeX interpretation (\input, \include)

meta.tip: \input substitutes and interprets the file contents. \include loads a .tex file (for somefile, somefile.tex will be loaded).
Inserts the raw file contents as-is, without interpreting LaTeX special characters.

entries.title: Deactivating special characters via \catcode (for files with $ # _ &)

meta.tip: If the injection point is after the document header (\usepackage cannot be used), some control characters can be deactivated to apply \input to a file containing $, #, _, &, null bytes, etc. (for example, perl scripts).

To bypass a blacklist, replace one character with its unicode hex value.
• ^^41 — uppercase A
• ^^7e — tilde (~); note that 'e' must be lowercase.
In the example ^^70 = 'p', so \lstin^^70utlisting -> \lstinputlisting.

entries.title: Executing a command via \write18 + reading the output

meta.tip: The command output goes to stdout, so the result must be saved to a temporary file and then read via \input. Requires compilation with shell-escape enabled (\write18).
If you get a LaTeX error, wrap the result in base64 to get rid of the bad characters (or use \verbatiminput).
From @EdOverflow. Triggers when the LaTeX link ends up in an HTML/PDF viewer that executes javascript:.
Works in MathJax (the unicode extension). Substitute your own JS for <ARBITRARY_JS_CODE>.
